headline: Iran in Freefall: Day 5 of Bombardment Leaves a Nation Without a Leader
subheadline: The killing of Supreme Leader Khamenei has created an unprecedented power vacuum as Operation Epic Fury dismantles Iran's military infrastructure

body.content: Five days into the most intensive air campaign since the 2003 invasion of Iraq, Iran finds itself in a condition that no modern state has experienced in peacetime memory: a country without a supreme leader, under continuous bombardment, with its economy in freefall and no clear path to political succession.

body.heading: Operation Epic Fury Enters Its Second Phase
body.content: The joint US-Israeli operation — dubbed Epic Fury by Washington and Roaring Lion by Jerusalem — has systematically dismantled Iran's military command structure over the past five days. Strikes have targeted IRGC command infrastructure, air defense networks, and nuclear facilities including the Natanz enrichment complex. The campaign's most consequential moment came on February 28, when Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei was killed in a strike that decapitated Iran's theocratic leadership. The head of state position remains constitutionally vacant. An IRGC Interim Leadership Council, comprising President Pezeshkian, judiciary chief Mohseni-Eje'i, and Assembly of Experts chairman Arafi, has assumed governing authority — but without the constitutional mandate that only the Supreme Leader holds. Iran's dual-authority system, split between republican and theocratic pillars, has never faced this scenario. The Assembly of Experts, the body constitutionally empowered to select a new supreme leader, cannot convene while strikes continue.

body.heading: An Economy in Collapse
body.content: Iran's economic indicators have deteriorated at a pace that surpasses even the worst scenarios modeled by international institutions. GDP is on a negative 10 to 15 percent annualized trajectory. Consumer price inflation has surged to 42.4 percent and is approaching hyperinflationary thresholds. The rial has collapsed on parallel markets, with the official rate becoming functionally meaningless. The political risk premium has been raised to 5,200 basis points — the highest of any country tracked in the Stratoterra dataset, exceeding even wartime Russia. Foreign exchange reserves, already depleted by years of sanctions, are being consumed at an accelerated rate as the central bank attempts to maintain basic import financing for food and medicine. The banking system is effectively non-functional for international transactions.

body.heading: IRGC Retaliates, But Capabilities Degrade
body.content: The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps has not been passive. Missile and drone strikes have targeted US military installations and Gulf state infrastructure across the region. The UAE, Kuwait, Qatar, and Saudi Arabia have all sustained direct attacks. But the IRGC's operational capacity is visibly degrading as command nodes are destroyed and communications infrastructure is severed. The key question for military analysts is how long Iran can sustain the Strait of Hormuz closure — its most potent remaining leverage — as its naval assets come under systematic targeting.

body.heading: Why This Matters for Investors
body.content: Iran itself has been effectively uninvestable for years under the sanctions regime, and the current conflict has merely formalized what was already the practical reality. The real investor concern is indirect and systemic. The duration of the Hormuz closure determines the trajectory of global energy prices, which in turn drives inflation expectations, central bank policy responses, and the creditworthiness of every energy-importing sovereign on the planet. Goldman Sachs has modeled Brent crude at $110 per barrel if disruptions persist into April, with JPMorgan's worst-case scenario at $120 to $130. Every additional week of closure compounds the stagflationary impulse that is already visible in bond markets and consumer sentiment surveys.

body.heading: What to Watch
body.content: Three variables will determine how this crisis evolves. First, the Assembly of Experts: any signal that Iran's clerical establishment is moving toward selecting a new supreme leader would indicate a path to political reconstitution. Second, the IRGC's naval capability in the Strait of Hormuz: satellite imagery and shipping data will reveal whether Iran's ability to maintain the blockade is eroding. Third, diplomatic channels: Turkey has positioned itself as a potential mediator, and any backchannel communication between Tehran's interim leadership and Washington would be the earliest indicator of a ceasefire trajectory. For investors, the actionable signal is the Hormuz reopening timeline — everything else follows from that.
